title: How Cinemas Taught Early Movie-Goers the Rules & Etiquette for Watching Films (1912): No Whistling, Standing or Wearing Big Hats
description:
in Film |   	July 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
I admit, I some┬Łtimes pay a pre┬Łmi┬Łum at a cer┬Łtain din┬Łner the┬Łater chain with a lob┬Łby-slash-b
body:
in Film |   	July 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
I admit, I some┬Łtimes pay a pre┬Łmi┬Łum at a cer┬Łtain din┬Łner the┬Łater chain with a lob┬Łby-slash-bar designed to look like clas┬Łsic indie video stores of yore. ItŌĆÖs not only the padded reclin┬Łers and half-decent grub that keeps me com┬Łing back. Nope, itŌĆÖs the rules. Print┬Łed on the menu are a list of dis┬Łrup┬Łtive behav┬Łiors that will get you uncer┬Łe┬Łmo┬Łni┬Łous┬Łly tossed outŌĆöno refunds and no back┬Łsies.
IŌĆÖve nev┬Łer seen it hap┬Łpen. Giv┬Łen what peo┬Łple put down for tick┬Łets, din┬Łner, drinks, and/or a babysit┬Łter, itŌĆÖs unlike┬Łly many risk blow┬Łing the evening. But know┬Łing that the the┬Łater takes silence seri┬Łous┬Łly brings seri┬Łous movie┬Łgo┬Łers peace of mind. What is a movie, after all, with┬Łout the all-impor┬Łtant dia┬Łlogue, music, and sound cues?

Well, itŌĆÖs silent film. And even then, when movies were sound-tracked with live accom┬Łpa┬Łni┬Łment and dia┬Łlogue appeared on title cards, peo┬Łple wor┬Łried very much about dis┬Łtrac┬Łtions. It just so hap┬Łpens that talk┬Łing and tex┬Łting (obvi┬Łous┬Łly) were the least of ear┬Łly audienceŌĆÖs con┬Łcerns.
For one thing, the cin┬Łe┬Łma was a place where class┬Łes, races, sex┬Łes, and ages ŌĆ£mixed much more freely than had been Vic┬Łto┬Łri┬Łan cus┬Łtom,ŌĆØ notes Rebec┬Łca Onion at Slate. There were the usu┬Łal con┬Łcerns about cor┬Łrup┬Łtion of the ŌĆ£del┬Łi┬Łcate sen┬Łsi┬Łbil┬Łi┬ŁtiesŌĆØ of ladies.

ŌĆ£But female cin┬Łe┬Łma-goers were just as like┬Łly to be seen as a prob┬Łlem,ŌĆØ writes Onion, ŌĆ£giv┬Łen their sup┬Łposed propen┬Łsi┬Łty for wear┬Łing big hats and chat┬Łting.ŌĆØ The melt┬Łing pot demo┬Łgraph┬Łic of the nick┬Łelodeon could be exhil┬Ła┬Łrat┬Łing, and audi┬Łence mem┬Łbers found they some┬Łtimes lost their inhi┬Łbi┬Łtions. ŌĆ£Some┬Łhow you enter into the spir┬Łit of the thing,ŌĆØ observed author W.W. Win┬Łters in 1910. ŌĆ£DonŌĆÖt you slip away from your┬Łself, lose your ret┬Łi┬Łcence, reserve, pride, and a few oth┬Łer things?ŌĆØ

These days weŌĆÖre accus┬Łtomed to cram┬Łming in elbow-to-elbow next to any┬Łone and every┬Łone, and we most┬Łly heed the onscreen cajol┬Łing to put our phones away and keep qui┬Łet, even when we arenŌĆÖt in spe┬Łcial┬Łty bou┬Łtique chains or local art┬Łhouse the┬Łaters. Then again, if cer┬Łtain behav┬Łiors werenŌĆÖt an issue, there wouldnŌĆÖt be ads pro┬Łhibit┬Łing them.

Enor┬Łmous hats and applause (and applause with things oth┬Łer than hands) may be relics of cinemaŌĆÖs infan┬Łcy. But swap out those admo┬Łni┬Łtions for oth┬Łers of the smart┬Łphone vari┬Łety and these lantern slides instruct┬Łing view┬Łers in 1912 about prop┬Łer movie the┬Łater eti┬Łquette donŌĆÖt look so dif┬Łfer┬Łent from todayŌĆ” sort of.

We might want for inter┬Łmis┬Łsions to return, espe┬Łcial┬Łly after the two-hour mark, and wouldnŌĆÖt it be nice if, instead of keep┬Łing us in our seats for post-cred┬Łit scenes, big block┬Łbuster movies just said ŌĆ£Good NightŌĆØ? See more of these delight┬Łful pub┬Łlic ser┬Łvice announce┬Łments from 1912 nick┬Łelodeons at Back Sto┬Łry Radio.

via Slate
Note: An ear┬Łli┬Łer ver┬Łsion of this post appeared on our site in 2019.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
The Art of Cre┬Łat┬Łing Spe┬Łcial Effects in Silent Movies: Inge┬Łnu┬Łity Before the Age of CGI
Enjoy the Great┬Łest Silent Films Ever Made in Our Col┬Łlec┬Łtion of 101 Free Silent Films Online
The Char┬Łlie Chap┬Łlin Archive Opens, Putting Online 30,000 Pho┬Łtos & Doc┬Łu┬Łments from the Life of the Icon┬Łic Film Star
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬Łcian based in Durham, NC.

title: TatlinŌĆÖs Tower, One of the Most Ambitious Buildings That Was Never Built
description:
in Architecture, Art, History |   	July 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

ItŌĆÖs no small project to found a new soci┬Łety, espe┬Łcial┬Łly when youŌĆÖr
body:
in Architecture, Art, History |   	July 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

ItŌĆÖs no small project to found a new soci┬Łety, espe┬Łcial┬Łly when youŌĆÖre doing it on the scale of a place like Rus┬Łsia. Apart from the con┬Łsid┬Łer┬Łable prac┬Łti┬Łcal chal┬Łlenges it entails, thereŌĆÖs also the need for sym┬Łbols bold enough to rep┬Łre┬Łsent the under┬Łly┬Łing ide┬Łal. The avant-garde artist Vladimir Tatlin took it upon him┬Łself to cre┬Łate just such a sym┬Łbol in the years after the Russ┬Łian Rev┬Ło┬Łlu┬Łtion. The result is offi┬Łcial┬Łly known as the Mon┬Łu┬Łment to the Third Inter┬Łna┬Łtion┬Łal, named for the orga┬Łni┬Łza┬Łtion tasked with the pro┬Łmo┬Łtion of world com┬Łmu┬Łnism (often abbre┬Łvi┬Łat┬Łed to Com┬Łintern). But itŌĆÖs more com┬Łmon┬Łly referred to as TatlinŌĆÖs Tow┬Łer, per┬Łhaps in trib┬Łute to the artistŌĆÖs par┬Łtic┬Łu┬Łlar vision ŌĆö one too ambi┬Łtious for its real-life con┬Łstruc┬Łtion even to begin.

ŌĆ£As part of a large-scale pro┬Łgram to replace old czarist mon┬Łu┬Łments with mon┬Łu┬Łments to the rev┬Ło┬Łlu┬Łtion, the huge struc┬Łture was both a sym┬Łbol┬Łic sculp┬Łture and func┬Łtion┬Łal archi┬Łtec┬Łture,ŌĆØ write Smarthis┬Łto┬ŁryŌĆÖs┬ĀCharles Cramer and Kim Grant. ŌĆ£Designed to strad┬Łdle the Neva riv┬Łer in St. Peters┬Łburg, the 1300 foot (400 meter) iron and glass Mon┬Łu┬Łment would sur┬Łpass ParisŌĆÖs Eif┬Łfel Tow┬Łer in both scale and com┬Łplex┬Łi┬Łty.ŌĆØ
Indeed, it would stand taller than the yet-to-be-con┬Łstruct┬Łed Empire State Build┬Łing, at least if you donŌĆÖt count its anten┬Łna. Con┬Łsist┬Łing of ŌĆ£a con┬Łtract┬Łing dou┬Łble helix that spi┬Łrals upward, sup┬Łport┬Łed by a huge diag┬Ło┬Łnal gird┬Łer,ŌĆØ TatlinŌĆÖs Tow┬Łer would con┬Łtain four sub-struc┬Łtures, each rotat┬Łing at a dif┬Łfer┬Łent speed.


	

Yes, rotat┬Łing, and ŌĆ£com┬Łplet┬Łing a full rev┬Ło┬Łlu┬Łtion in accor┬Łdance with the impor┬Łtance of the insti┬Łtu┬Łtions con┬Łduct┬Łing their busi┬Łness on the inside,ŌĆØ as Tim Brinkof writes at Big Think. ŌĆ£The cube that con┬Łtains the leg┬Łis┬Łla┬Łture would have com┬Łplet┬Łed a full rota┬Łtion once per year. The pyra┬Łmid above, hous┬Łing the offices of par┬Łty exec┬Łu┬Łtives, would have need┬Łed a month. The infor┬Łma┬Łtion cen┬Łter, locat┬Łed at the very peak, would have rotat┬Łed once a day, offer┬Łing a 360-degree view of Pet┬Łro┬Łgrad,ŌĆØ as St. Peters┬Łburg was known in 1920. (It would be re-named Leningrad in 1924 before going back to St. Peters┬Łburg in 1991, after the end of the Sovi┬Łet era.) You can learn more about how it all worked from the Archi┬Łtec┬Łture Enthu┬Łsi┬Łast video at the top of the post, and the one from Side┬Łpro┬Łjects just above.

ŌĆ£TatlinŌĆÖs Tow┬Łer was designed dur┬Łing a time when Com┬Łmu┬Łnist rule was still nascent and par┬Łty lead┬Łers sought to estab┬Łlish a new and dis┬Łtinct┬Łly social┬Łist iden┬Łti┬Łty through art,ŌĆØ writes Brinkof. Ide┬Łal┬Łized rep┬Łre┬Łsen┬Łta┬Łtions of the rul┬Łing class hav┬Łing been aggres┬Łsive┬Łly scrapped along with the rul┬Łing class itself, the Bol┬Łshe┬Łviks wel┬Łcomed any style that could shore up their rev┬Ło┬Łlu┬Łtion┬Łary cause, total abstrac┬Łtion includ┬Łed. Alas, though many par┬Łty offi┬Łcials approved of TatlinŌĆÖs design, they com┬Łmand┬Łed noth┬Łing like the resources to build it: ŌĆ£Rus┬Łsia would go bank┬Łrupt if it tried to acquire the insane amounts of steel and iron need┬Łed for the tow┬ŁerŌĆÖs skele┬Łtal frame┬Łwork.ŌĆØ Per┬Łhaps Leon Trot┬Łsky, one of the pro┬Łjec┬ŁtŌĆÖs dis┬Łsenters, was right when he called it ŌĆ£imprac┬Łti┬Łcal and roman┬ŁticŌĆØ ŌĆö and per┬Łhaps those are the very qual┬Łi┬Łties that keep TatlinŌĆÖs Tow┬Łer an object of fas┬Łci┬Łna┬Łtion more than a cen┬Łtu┬Łry lat┬Łer.
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
The Utopi┬Łan, Social┬Łist Designs of Sovi┬Łet Cities
Every┬Łthing You Need to Know About Mod┬Łern Russ┬Łian Art in 25 Min┬Łutes: A Visu┬Łal Intro┬Łduc┬Łtion to Futur┬Łism, Social┬Łist Real┬Łism & More
What Makes Kaz┬Łimir MalevichŌĆÖs Black Square (1915) Not Just Art, But Impor┬Łtant Art
The Unre┬Łal┬Łized Projects of Frank Lloyd Wright Get Brought to Life with 3D Dig┬Łi┬Łtal Recon┬Łstruc┬Łtions
An Intro┬Łduc┬Łtion to Bru┬Łtal┬Łism: the Icon┬Łic Post┬Łwar Archi┬Łtec┬Łtur┬Łal Style That Com┬Łbined Utopi┬Łanism and Con┬Łcrete
The Futur┬Łist Archi┬Łtec┬Łtur┬Łal Designs Cre┬Łat┬Łed by ├ēti┬Łenne-Louis Boul┬Łl├®e in the 18th Cen┬Łtu┬Łry
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Korea's Coastal Folklore Surfaces in Jeongmin Lee's Ink Illustrations
description: Jeongmin Lee is interested in the ways ŌĆ£memory is carried through craft and repetition.ŌĆØ On traditional Korean mulberry paper, or hanji, Lee draws
body:
Jeongmin Lee is interested in the ways ŌĆ£memory is carried through craft and repetition.ŌĆØ On traditional Korean mulberry paper, or hanji, Lee draws delicate lines in ink and pigments known as bunchae, rendering rippling textures that whirl across the page. Steeped in local folklore and mythology, the Busan-based artist creates surreal scenes that conjure fantastical tales of life by the sea.
ŌĆ£Most of my recent projects begin with reading regional folktales, visiting places connected to those stories, and collecting fragments of history, mythology, and oral traditions,ŌĆØ she says. ŌĆ£I rarely paint a folktale exactly as itŌĆÖs written; IŌĆÖm more interested in its symbols, emotions, and the questions it leaves behind.ŌĆØ

Many of LeeŌĆÖs compositions focus on womenŌĆÖs knowledge, labor, and resilience and how those qualities emerge through coastal storytelling. Tales of powerful sea gods and the diving traditions of the haenyeo commingle into balanced illustrations that translate the symbols and motifs of the region anew.
While her approach is labor-intensive, Lee enjoys utilizing such a meticulous, meditative technique. ŌĆ£Painting with traditional pigments requires a slow, layered process. The colors are built gradually, allowing the paper and pigments to create subtle textures that wouldnŌĆÖt be possible with other materials,ŌĆØ she adds. ŌĆ£It gives me space to sit with these stories while I paint them.
Many of the works shown here are part of Daughters of the Sea, an ongoing series recently on view at the S─ĆBRS Festival in Riga. Lee hopes to expand the project into one thatŌĆÖs participatory and connects similar narratives from across the globe. ŌĆ£IŌĆÖm exploring ways that folklore can become something people experience and talk about together, rather than something that exists only on a gallery wall,ŌĆØ Lee shares.
SheŌĆÖs also currently working on a graphic novel centering on BusanŌĆÖs mythology and folklore, which she hopes to complete next year. Keep up with her projects on Instagram.

schema.headline: Korea's Coastal Folklore Surfaces in Jeongmin Lee's Ink Illustrations

title: Uncanny Landscapes in Pen and Ink Span Wooden Panels by John Buck
description: From the Three Patriarchs of Zion Canyon to the swamps of Louisiana to the immense cascade of Niagara Falls, John BuckŌĆÖs dreamlike landscapes evok
body:
From the Three Patriarchs of Zion Canyon to the swamps of Louisiana to the immense cascade of Niagara Falls, John BuckŌĆÖs dreamlike landscapes evoke the juxtapositions and proportions of dreams. His solo exhibition, Mont Blanc on Wood at Zolla / Lieberman Gallery, draws us to the fuzzy boundary between the familiar and the uncanny.
The Bozeman-based artist is known for his eccentric, often life-size wooden sculptures that draw on folklore, personal memory, and daily observations. Figures are sometimes hybridized with other objects, and idiosyncratic drawings on wood panel reveal expansive landscapes populated by anthropomorphized plants and dramatic rock pinnacles.
ŌĆ£LighthouseŌĆØ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 27 x 36 ┬Į inches
The works in Mont Blanc on Wood span a range of places and references, emphasizing landscapes where things feel perhaps a little ŌĆ£off.ŌĆØ The desert stares back in ŌĆ£Cactus Grove (No. 13),ŌĆØ and in ŌĆ£Lighthouse (No. 18),ŌĆØ a light station in the middle of a desert lake shines mostly onto arid, rocky landforms.
ŌĆ£Buck reflects on social and political realities, environmental concerns, and the eccentricities of human behavior, all while maintaining a sense of humor and a deep engagement with craft,ŌĆØ the gallery says. ŌĆ£His art balances storytelling with formal clarity, inviting viewers into a world where the familiar becomes speculative and symbolic.ŌĆØ
Mont Blanc on Wood continues through August 8 in Chicago.
ŌĆ£Cactus GroveŌĆØ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 36 ┬Į x 27 inches
ŌĆ£Three Patriarchs, UtahŌĆØ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 26 ┬Į x 31 ┬Į inches
ŌĆ£The Never Sweat MineŌĆØ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 21 ┬╝ x 26 ┬Į inches
ŌĆ£AtchafalayaŌĆØ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 26 ┬Į x 32 inches
ŌĆ£7 Waterfalls (No14),ŌĆØ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 37 x 27 inches
ŌĆ£AspensŌĆØ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 26 ┬Į x 37 ┬Į inches
ŌĆ£Sea MountŌĆØ (2024), pen and ink on wood, 52 x 37 ┬Į inches

title: Helena Minginowicz Transforms Humble Paper Towel into Ethereal Paintings
description: ŌĆ£Civilizations are remembered through their monuments, but understood through the things they throw away,ŌĆØ says artist Helena Minginowicz, whose s
body:
ŌĆ£Civilizations are remembered through their monuments, but understood through the things they throw away,ŌĆØ says artist Helena Minginowicz, whose sensitive paintings interrogate our understanding of value. Using airbrushed acrylic, which can be built up in lightweight, translucent layers, the artist takes one of the most quotidian household items as a starting point: paper towel.
With its machine-embossed, moisture-wicking patterns, the absorbent paper comprises an instantly recognizable substrate. The precise, textured flourishes are aesthetically pleasing, and yet itŌĆÖs hard to completely separate them from our associations with mass-produced paper products that are designed for one-time use and disposability. This dichotomy sits at he heart of MinginowiczŌĆÖs practice, in which she explores ŌĆ£how changing the hierarchy of materials can reshape the way we perceive value, dignity, and the human experience,ŌĆØ she tells Colossal.

MinginowiczŌĆÖs embossed pieces from everyday domestic material are one facet of a broader multimedia approach to materiality in which she creates paintings on canvas and also painstakingly embosses delicate tissues. The paper towel works, in addition to some that are made on supermarket-style plastic bags, are then presented between thick slabs of acrylic, transforming them into objects with substantial heft and dimensionality.
ŌĆ£Every civilization constructs its own hierarchy of values,ŌĆØ the artist says. ŌĆ£It decides what deserves to be preserved, admired, and passed on to future generations. Monuments, works of art, symbols, and myths preserve an image of humanity as we wish to remember itŌĆöstrong, beautiful, enduring, and heroic. Yet every monument has its reverse.ŌĆØ
MinginowiczŌĆÖ imagery draws on the style of Renaissance paintings, especially focusing on expressiveness, intimacy, and the idealized female figure. One might think of Sandro BotticelliŌĆÖs ŌĆ£The Birth of VenusŌĆØ or aristocratic portraiture of the era. ŌĆ£For centuries, painting monumentalized what civilizations wished to remember: saints, heroes, gods, victories, myths, and ideals. I use that same language to ask a different question: Who deserves to be remembered with dignity? Not only heroes. Not only the victorious. But every human being.ŌĆØ
Minginowicz is currently working toward a solo exhibition at Galerie Prima in Paris, which is slated to open on October 8. Follow updates and see more on Instagram.

schema.headline: Helena Minginowicz Transforms Humble Paper Towel into Ethereal Paintings

title: Should We See Peter HujarŌĆÖs Contact Sheets?
description:
Art Review
                Though not made for public consumption, the darkroom work prints reveal his portraits as excerpts of
body:
Art Review
                Though not made for public consumption, the darkroom work prints reveal his portraits as excerpts of a conversation, rather than memories snatched from the ether.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Peter Hujar, "The Cockettes at 10 East 23rd Street, 1971, job 519" (┬® The Peter Hujar Archive / Artists Rights Society (ARS); courtesy Fraenkel Gallery, San Francisco and Ortuzar, New York; all other photos Julia Curl/Hyperallergic)The Morgan Library & Museum might be milking its Peter Hujar collection, but IŌĆÖm not complaining. Hujar:Contact is the MorganŌĆÖs second solo exhibition of the photographerŌĆÖs work, coming on the heels of its 2013 acquisition of his archive and a sweeping 2018 retrospective, Peter Hujar: Speed of Life. That exhibition was always going to be a tough act to follow, given the scale of the show, the impact of its reception, and HujarŌĆÖs importance as a key portraitist of New York CityŌĆÖs before-they-were-famous queer art scene. Nevertheless, itŌĆÖs an unusual choice to mount an exhibition entirely dedicated to a photographerŌĆÖs contact sheets, which by nature are not made for public consumption. For those of us more fluent in digital culture, contact sheets are darkroom work prints that allow photographers to see all the images on a roll of film and select which ones are worthy of printing, given the time and expense that full-size printing requires ŌĆö essentially, itŌĆÖs the equivalent of showing us screenshots of HujarŌĆÖs camera roll. As eager as I am for a nerdy deep dive into his process, which the exhibition undoubtedly is, IŌĆÖm left wrestling with the vague unease that it might also be an opportunistic mining of the photographerŌĆÖs archive for all its worth. But IŌĆÖll check my paranoia at the door, because either way, the showŌĆÖs outcome is good.From left to right: Peter Hujar, ŌĆ£Candy Darling, Cabrini Health Care CenterŌĆØ (1973, Job 587), 6 sheets; ŌĆ£Candy Darling, Cabrini Health Care CenterŌĆØ (1973), Job 587, 6 sheets, ŌĆ£Candy Darling on Her DeathbedŌĆØ (1973), gelatin silver printEntering the exhibition, the viewer is immersed on all sides in mural-sized photographic blowups of HujarŌĆÖs contact sheets and notebooks ŌĆö a wise move, offsetting the small scale of the contact sheets (all 8.5 x 11 inches, or ~21.6 x 28 cm) and inviting one in for a closer look. (The trough full of magnifying glasses to borrow is also a nice touch). The introductory text is clearly prepared to reassure the cynic, stating at the outset that Hujar himself saved these materials for future scholars to study and ŌĆ£learn how I got to the final print.ŌĆØ The Morgan is, of course, a library and a museum, which strengthens the argument for its archive-forward approach.Visually, itŌĆÖs more of a mixed bag, as contact sheets so often are. They are most interesting as objects themselves when the photographs on the film strips come together to form a broader image or pattern ŌĆö as in his nudes ŌĆ£Andrew, English boyŌĆØ (c. 1972) and ŌĆ£Bruce de Sainte CroixŌĆØ (1976) ŌĆö or when the sheet appears to have inspired a repetition in the final print, like in ŌĆ£Ray Johnson TwiceŌĆØ (c. 1966). Hujar, fortunately, did most of his shooting on medium-format 120 film, meaning that the negatives are larger (2.4 x 2.4 inches or 6 x 6 cm) and nicer to look at as a grid; the handful of contact sheets for his 35mm work (a smaller, rectangular format) are harder to appreciate. Left: Peter Hujar, ŌĆ£Ray Johnson, Henry Geldzahler, Joseph Raffael, and Harold Krieger studio staffŌĆØ (c. 1966), Job 296, 2 sheets, contact sheet; right: ŌĆ£Ray Johnson TwiceŌĆØ (c. 1966), gelatin silver printThe photographerŌĆÖs annotations ŌĆö dotting or circling his favorites, marking where to crop ŌĆö occasionally provide a glimpse into his evolving thought process. In ŌĆ£Seven-part movement executed by Robyn Brentano, S. K. Dunn, and Charles Dennis of Robert WilsonŌĆÖs Byrd Hoffman School of ByrdsŌĆØ (1973), for example, Hujar numbers the images in a sequence that differs from the order in which he took the photographs, omitting some and reordering others to construct a smoother visual narrative.┬ĀThese notes-to-self helped him choose which images to make full-size (and thus more expensive) prints of later, and many such photographs hang nearby, like HujarŌĆÖs eternally moving 1973 portrait of Candy Darling on her deathbed alongside its contact sheet.One walks away with the impression that, for Peter Hujar, photography was never a matter of the ŌĆ£decisive moment.ŌĆØ The contact sheets reveal that his portraits were the product of ongoing interactions ŌĆö excerpts of a conversation, rather than memories snatched from the ether. Sometimes, they betray the inherent awkwardness of trying to coax someone, even a friend, into revealing themselves for the camera. ItŌĆÖs often harder to let down oneŌĆÖs guard than oneŌĆÖs clothes, and Hujar managed to capture his subjects doing both.Installation view of Hujar:Contact Left: Peter Hujar, ŌĆ£Andrew, English boyŌĆØ (c. 1972), Job 550, 16 sheets, contact sheet; right: ŌĆ£Jay and FernandoŌĆØ (1967), Job 322, 14 sheets, contact sheetInstallation view of Hujar:Contact Left: Magnifying glasses for viewers to use to examine Hujar's contact sheets; installation view of Hujar:Contact Hujar:Contact continues at the Morgan Library & Museum (225 Madison Avenue, Midtown, Manhattan) through October 25. The exhibition was curated by Joel Smith. 
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Review
                      
                      
                        Peter Hujar
                      
                      
                        Morgan Library & Museum
                      
                      
                        New York

schema.headline: Should We See Peter HujarŌĆÖs Contact Sheets?

title: "Pam the Bird" Graffiti Stirs Controversy in Melbourne
description:
News
                Days after police arrested the graffiti artist who tagged a prominent bridge tower with a giant bird, some are c
body:
News
                Days after police arrested the graffiti artist who tagged a prominent bridge tower with a giant bird, some are calling on the city to preserve the work. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Aerial view of bird graffiti painted on Bolte Bridge pillar in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia, July 9, 2026 (photo GoAerials via Shutterstock)An enormous graffiti bird in Melbourne, Australia, has stirred considerable controversy as the public and officials contend with the artwork's future.Days after police arrested a man who had scaled and tagged a prominent Melbourne bridge with a cartoon bird dubbed "Pam the Bird," thousands have called upon the city to preserve the graffiti artwork. The Victoria Police have cast 22-year-old graffiti artist Jack Gibson-Burrell as a "notorious vandal" whose tactics have recently escalated into alleged violence, and charged him with multiple offenses for his most recent stunt. Meanwhile, nearly 9,000 petitioners have defended his "Pam the Bird" display as a work of "spontaneous urban culture" that ought to be preserved.On July 7, Gibson-Burrell surrendered to police following a tense standoff during which the artist sat perched atop a 140-foot tower on Melbourne's Bolte Bridge. The artist allegedly forced a door to a staircase that led to the top of the landmark tower, where he abseiled to tag an enormous upside-down bird and the text "Fuck You."Gibson-Burrell currently faces over 200 charges, the majority of which are related to his prolific graffiti work around Melbourne. The artist has allegedly tagged "Pam the Bird" on other major buildings in the city over the past couple of years, including the clock tower of the Flinders Street railway station. He also faces separate charges related to allegedly assaulting a retail worker. He was reportedly out on bail at the time of the Bolte Bridge incident and living with his grandmother.On the Australian petition-building website GetUp, signatories called upon Melbourne officials to preserve ŌĆö rather than remove ŌĆö Bolte Bridge's "Pam the Bird," citing its wide popularity. Removing the tag from the tower would cost the city tens of thousands of Australian dollars, according to local news outlets. The petition states that its advocacy for the embattled avian caricature is not intended to encourage similar illegal stunts in the future."When [something like this] happens, perhaps the best response isn't to immediately paint over it," reads the petition, authored by Eric Giordmaina. "But to acknowledge that public art sometimes appears in unexpected ways."Police responded to the incident at Bolte Bridge after 2am on July 7. In photographs of the scene, Gibson-Burrell can be seen delivering a black-gloved middle finger to the police waiting for him at the bottom of the concrete structure. Around 11 am, he surrendered with his hands up on a nearby embankment. On a now-deleted Instagram account believed to belong to Gibson-Burrell, entitled "pambirdofficial," a masked man says, "I'm not coming down until they lower the taxes. I'm fucking sick of paying that shit."Hyperallergic attempted to contact Gibson-Burrell, Giordmaina, and the city of Melbourne for comment.
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        Melbourne
                      
                      
                        Australia
                      
                      
                        Graffiti

schema.headline: "Pam the Bird" Graffiti Stirs Controversy in Melbourne
